Source_Species.reason: The study organism is Epichloë festucae strain Fl1.

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The paper repeatedly names the fungus as Epichloë festucae.

Source_Species_Variety.reason: Methods 2.1 states E. festucae strain Fl1 (ex-cultivar SR3000) was the wild type strain.

Applied_Species.reason: The work evaluates infected perennial ryegrass plants in endophyte symbiosis.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: Perennial ryegrass is the host and the endophyte is E. festucae; Methods specifies perennial ryegrass seedlings inoculated with Fl1-derived strains.

Applied_Species_Variety.reason: Methods 2.2 states perennial ryegrass cultivar GA66 (endophyte-free) seedlings were used for inoculation.

Enzyme_Name.reason: GigA is described as the precursor gene/protein for epichloecyclins, i.e., a RiPP precursor peptide rather than an enzyme.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: Results 3.1 says no conserved domains were detected for GigA, and Figure 1 shows a signal sequence and multiple core peptides consistent with a secreted multicore RiPP precursor.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: gigA is directly studied as part of the epichloecyclin biosynthetic gene cluster.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: GigA is the precursor peptide from which epichloecyclin core peptides derive, fitting precursor formation.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: GigA acts as the precursor at the beginning of the pathway.

Primary_Product.reason: Figure 1 shows GigA protein with four core peptides, i.e., a propeptide containing four core peptide repeats.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: The paper states epichloecyclins are produced from the single gene gigA and Figure 1/Discussion describe processing of GigA into smaller peptide units.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: Results show ΔgigA abolished epichloecyclins, so it is clearly required; the statement explicitly notes rate-limiting was not established.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: GigA directly encodes the precursor peptide for epichloecyclins.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: gigA disruption abolished methylated epichloecyclins and desmethylepichloecyclins were not detected in ΔgigA, but the paper only detected desmethylepichloecyclins A-D in WT and B-D in ΔkexB; desmethylepichloecyclin E was not generally observed outside ΔgigC contexts, so listing A-E for both forms overstates support.

Terminal_Metabolite_Class.reason: Epichloecyclins are explicitly described as cyclic peptides/RiPPs.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: Chemistry analyses used freeze-dried aboveground herbage, and peptide analyses mention ground pseudostem tissue.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: Results 3.3 and Table 2 show ΔgigA abolished epichloecyclins and ΔgigA/gigA partially restored some products.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All three statements are supported by Results 3.1 and 3.3 plus Discussion on partial complementation.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: Results 3.3 states symbiosis is required to activate expression of the grass-induced genes (gig).

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: GigA function was evaluated during endophytic growth in infected ryegrass plants.

Subcellular_Localization.reason: Figure 1 shows a signal peptide, supporting secretion/propeptide export.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: The study used gene disruption, complementation, and LC-MS profiling in infected plants.

Applied_Promoters.reason: Complementation with full-length gene was performed, but the exact promoter usage is not explicitly stated as native promoter for gigA.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: The phenotype/metabolite work was done in planta under glasshouse growth conditions.

Omics_Data.reason: Results 3.1 explicitly says transcriptome sequences were used to determine gene boundaries; no accession is provided.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: gigA is clustered/functionally related with GigB, GigC, and KexB, but direct physical interactions are not shown.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: Results/Discussion indicate no gross host phenotype change for ΔgigA and negligible overall influence on other alkaloids, despite some specific alkaloid differences in ΔgigA/gigA.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: This is a forward-looking application inference; the paper discusses future activation/engineering opportunities only indirectly and does not frame nutritional or breeding value.

Other_Important_Info.reason: Table 1 gives GigA accession ACB30126.1, Figure 1 shows signal peptide and LXKR sites, and Discussion notes native genomic context may matter for full activity.

Gene_Accession_Number.reason: Table 1 lists GigB GenBank ID QPH04226.1.

Source_Species.reason: gigB is from Epichloë festucae strain Fl1.

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The paper names the source fungus as Epichloë festucae.

Applied_Species.reason: gigB was tested in perennial ryegrass-endophyte symbiosis.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The host is perennial ryegrass (Lolium perenne) infected with E. festucae.

Applied_Species_Variety.reason: GA66 endophyte-free host seedlings were inoculated with Fl1-derived strains.

Enzyme_Name.reason: The paper concludes GigB is associated with cyclization of epichloecyclins, so calling it an epichloecyclin cyclase is consistent.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: Results 3.1 explicitly identifies a DUF3328 domain in GigB.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: gigB is part of the epichloecyclin biosynthetic cluster.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: GigB is implicated in RiPP cyclization.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: Cyclization is an intermediate pathway step after precursor processing and before/with final maturation.

Primary_Substrate.reason: The paper infers GigB acts on processed linear GigA-derived intermediates, but these substrates were not directly detected.

Primary_Product.reason: Loss of gigB abolished cyclic epichloecyclins and complementation restored them, supporting cyclized epichloecyclins as the product.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: Results/Discussion support GigB as responsible for cyclization, likely involving conserved tyrosine linkage to the N-terminal residue, though exact chemistry remains partly speculative.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: Essential for cyclic product formation is supported by ΔgigB abolishing all cyclic epichloecyclins; 'key control step' is reasonable, but not quantitatively rate-limiting.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: gigB is directly required to form the cyclic target products.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: Results 3.3 and Table 2 support connection to epichloecyclins A-E.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: Metabolites were quantified from aboveground herbage, and pseudostem tissue was also analyzed in related assays.

Terminal_Metabolite_Function.reason: The paper repeatedly states ecological role is unknown and speculates they may act as bioactives.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: Table 2 shows ΔgigB lacked cyclic epichloecyclins and ΔgigB/gigB restored them.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All four points are supported: DUF3328 requirement for cyclization, absence in ΔgigB, restoration in complement, and increased tiller number/biomass with a height trend.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: gig genes are grass-induced, and Results 3.1 mentions coexpression/cluster context.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: Functional evaluation was performed in infected ryegrass symbiosis.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: The study used disruption, complementation, LC-MS, and attempted peptidomics for intermediates.

Applied_Promoters.reason: Complementation was done with PCR products/cotransformation, but the paper does not explicitly state promoter identity for gigB complementation.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: gigB function was studied in planta in glasshouse-grown perennial ryegrass.

Omics_Data.reason: Transcriptome sequences were used for annotation; peptidomics and LC-MS metabolomics were also used.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: GigA, GigC, and KexB are pathway partners, but no direct protein-protein interaction evidence is provided.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: Results show ΔgigB increased tiller number and biomass; no fungal morphology defect was observed except for ΔkexB.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: This is a speculative application statement not directly supported by the Results.

Other_Important_Info.reason: The paper explicitly states linear intermediates were not detected and that the strongest evidence supports GigB involvement in cyclization.

Gene_Accession_Number.reason: Table 1 lists GigC GenBank ID QPH04223.1.

Source_Species.reason: gigC is from Epichloë festucae strain Fl1.

Applied_Species.reason: gigC was tested in perennial ryegrass-endophyte symbiosis.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The host-endophyte combination is Lolium perenne + Epichloë festucae.

Enzyme_Name.reason: The paper concludes GigC is the methyltransferase responsible for lysine methylation of epichloecyclins.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: Results 3.1 states GigC contains a SET domain.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: gigC is part of the epichloecyclin biosynthetic cluster.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: GigC functions in methylation of the RiPP products.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: Discussion infers methylation can occur last, making it a downstream tailoring step.

Primary_Substrate.reason: Results/Discussion show ΔgigC accumulates desmethylepichloecyclins, supporting these as GigC substrates.

Primary_Product.reason: GigC is responsible for methylated epichloecyclins, including dimethylated forms.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: The paper explicitly attributes mono-/dimethylation of the conserved lysine residue to GigC.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: It is clearly a required tailoring step for methylated final products; no claim of kinetic rate limitation is made.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: gigC is required for methylation state of the target epichloecyclins.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: Results connect gigC to both methylated epichloecyclins and desmethylepichloecyclins measured in Table 2.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: These metabolites were measured in aboveground herbage; pseudostem tissue was also used for related analyses.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: ΔgigC lacked methylated epichloecyclins and accumulated desmethylepichloecyclins; complementation partially restored methylated products.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: Each statement is supported by Results 3.1, 3.3, Table 2, and Discussion.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: gig genes are grass-induced/in planta and clustered/coregulated with gigA.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: Function was assessed in infected ryegrass symbiosis.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: Gene disruption, complementation, and LC-MS profiling of methylated/demethylated metabolites were used.

Applied_Promoters.reason: Complementation was performed, but promoter identity is not explicitly reported.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: In planta symbiosis under glasshouse growth is the tested condition.

Omics_Data.reason: Transcriptome sequences were used for annotation and LC-MS metabolomics for product analysis.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: GigA, GigB, and KexB are pathway-related, but no direct interaction evidence is presented.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: Results show increased tiller number for ΔgigC-infected plants and overall negligible influence on other alkaloids.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: This engineering-use statement is plausible but not directly tested in the paper.

Other_Important_Info.reason: Discussion notes partial complementation and possible dependence on genomic context for full activity/expression.

Gene_Accession_Number.reason: Table 1 lists KexB GenBank ID ACB30127.1.

Source_Species.reason: kexB is from Epichloë festucae strain Fl1.

Source_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The source fungus is Epichloë festucae.

Applied_Species.reason: kexB was tested in perennial ryegrass-endophyte symbiosis.

Applied_Species_Latin_Name.reason: The applied system is Lolium perenne infected with E. festucae.

Applied_Species_Variety.reason: GA66 endophyte-free seedlings were used for inoculation.

Enzyme_Name.reason: The abstract and Results describe KexB as a subtilisin-like enzyme/protease.

Protein_Family_or_Domain.reason: Results 3.1 explicitly states KexB has a peptidase S8 domain.

Biosynthetic_Pathway.reason: kexB is part of the epichloecyclin biosynthetic cluster.

Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway.reason: KexB functions in proteolytic processing of the RiPP precursor.

Metabolic_Step_Position.reason: Proteolytic cleavage precedes/overlaps later maturation, so upstream to midstream is consistent.

Primary_Substrate.reason: Figure 1 and Discussion indicate GigA contains LXKR/kexin recognition sites putatively cleaved by kexin, supporting GigA propeptide as substrate.

Primary_Product.reason: The paper supports cleaved peptide intermediates required for maturation, and specifically epichloecyclin A processing depends on KexB.

Catalyzed_Reaction_Description.reason: Abstract, Figure 1, and Discussion support proteolytic cleavage of GigA/intermediates at kexin-like sites during maturation.

EC_Number.reason: No EC number for KexB is provided in the paper.

Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step.reason: The paper supports a specialized but partially redundant processing role, especially because B-E remain in ΔkexB while A is lost.

End_Product_Connection_Type.reason: KexB forms processing intermediates immediately upstream of final epichloecyclins.

Terminal_Metabolite.reason: Results connect kexB to the epichloecyclin A-E product profile.

Terminal_Metabolite_Accumulation_Site.reason: Products were measured in aboveground herbage; pseudostem was used in related assays.

Core_Phenotypic_Effect.reason: Table 2 shows ΔkexB lacked epichloecyclin A but retained near-WT B-E and complementation restored A.

Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene.reason: All four points are supported by Results 3.1, 3.3, 3.4, Table 2, and Figure 6.

Expression_Pattern_of_Source_Species.reason: The gig cluster genes including kexB are described as grass-induced and coexpressed with gigA.

Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species.reason: Functional testing occurred in infected ryegrass symbiosis.

Subcellular_Localization.reason: Discussion speculates production/processing could occur in the Golgi apparatus, but this is not direct evidence for KexB localization.

Core_Validation_Method.reason: Gene disruption, complementation, LC-MS profiling, and confocal microscopy were used.

Applied_Promoters.reason: Complementation is described but promoter usage is not explicitly stated.

Environment_or_Treatment_Factor.reason: The experiments were performed in planta in glasshouse-grown perennial ryegrass.

Omics_Data.reason: Transcriptome sequences were used for annotation and LC-MS metabolomics for products.

Interacting_Proteins.reason: GigA substrate relationship and possible redundancy with KexA are supported, but direct interaction evidence is lacking.

Potential_Tradeoffs.reason: Results show abnormal in planta hyphal growth for ΔkexB, significantly lower lolitrem B/F, and lower but non-significant biomass trend.

Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu.reason: This is a speculative application interpretation not directly tested by the paper.

Other_Important_Info.reason: Discussion explicitly suggests partial redundancy with KexA and specific dependence of epichloecyclin A on KexB.

corrections.reason: No EC number for KexB is provided in the paper.
